coredata.dc:title: Single Li ion conducting solid-state polymer electrolytes based on carbon quantum dots for Li-metal batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zeyu
Liu, Feng
Chen, Shaoshan
Zhai, Fei
Li, Yu
Feng, Yiyu
Feng, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Single Li ion conducting solid-state polymer electrolytes (SLIC-SPEs) can effectively inhibit the growth of Li dendrites in Li-metal batteries. However, SLIC-SPE synthesis using traditional polymerization methods yields electrolytes with insufficient conductivity, which limits their practical application. Herein, a novel Li+ conductor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is fabricated via the pyrolysis of poly(lithium 4-styrene sulfonate) and citric acid. The large CQD anionic size and hydrogen bonding interaction with matrix hinder anion migration in the polyethylene oxide matrix and thereby grants a high Li+ transport number of 0.9446. Moreover, CQD incorporation improves the mechanical properties and ionic conductivity of the SPEs. The as-prepared SPE membrane demonstrates a high room-temperature ionic conductivity of 2.02 × 10−4 S cm−1. All-solid-state Li-metal batteries fabricated with these SPEs show good cycling stability, rate performance, and capacity retention over 1000 cycles at 2 C and 60 °C. The SPEs also withstand deformations such as bending and twisting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Single-ion conductor
Solid-state polymer electrolytes
Li-metal batteries

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic of preparation process for PLSSCQD. b) TEM image of PLSSCQD. Inset: HRTEM image showing lattice fringe. c) Size distribution of PLSSCQD particles and d) Emission spectra of aqueous PLSSCQD shown at different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm). e) XPS survey spectrum.

XPS spectra of PLSSCQD in the energy range of a) C 1s and b) S 2p signal. c) FTIR spectra of PLSSCQD, PEO and PLSSCQD/PEO. SEM image of PLSSCQD-4/PEO membrane d) surface and e) cross-section, and EDS mapping of C, O, and S.

Microstructure and characteristics of the prepared membranes. PLM images of a) pure PEO and b) PLSSCQD-4/PEO. c) Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) profiles of different membranes. d) Schematic of the LMB using PLSSCQD/PEO SPE.

Mechanical properties of the prepared membranes. a) Photographs of PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE film before and after folding, coiling, twisting, and unfolding. b) Tensile stress–strain curves of different membranes. Inset: photo of stretched sample. c) Puncture-resistance stress–strain curves of different membranes. Inset: photo of punctured sample.

Electrochemical characterization of different SPEs. a) Temperature dependent δ Li + of SPEs from 25° to 80°C. b) Chronoamperometry profiles and AC impedance spectra before and after polarization for symmetric Li cells with PLSSCQD-4/PEO at 60 °C. c) LSV of PLSSCQD/PEO, PLSS/PEO, and LiTFSI/PEO electrolytes at a sweep rate of 1 mV s−1. d) Electrochemical floating analysis of PLSSCQD-4/PEO, LiTFSI/PEO, and PLSS/PEO using NCM523 cathode. e) Galvanostatic cycling for electrolytes in symmetric Li cells at 0.2, 0.5, and 1 mA cm−2 and 60 °C.

Discharge–charge profiles of LFP|SPE|Li cells: a) at 0.1 C and different cycles; b) at various rates and 60 °C. c) Capacity and columbic efficiency versus cycle number of the cells at 0.2 C and 60 °C. d) Rate capacities of batteries with different SPEs at 60 °C.

SEM images showing the morphology of Li. a) Initial bare Li. Electrodeposited Li when using b) PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE and c) LiTFSI/PEO SPE after 100 cycles of discharge–charge at a current density of 0.2 C.

a) Photo images of PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE film being abused via, twisting and unfolding, then assembling in a coin cell lighting an LED bulb. b) LFP|PLSSCQD-4|Li pouch cell lighting an LED bulb during folding and unfolding at 60 °C.
Physiochemical data for the phase behaviors of the solid electrolytes.

head.ce:title: Single Li ion conducting solid-state polymer electrolytes based on carbon quantum dots for Li-metal batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Ceramics and Machining Technology Ministry of Education, Tianjin 300072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Ceramics and Machining Technology Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Ceramics and Machining Technology Ministry of Education, Tianjin 300072, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Materials Processing and Mold, Ministry of Education, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Materials Processing and Mold, Ministry of Education, Zhengzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Materials Processing and Mold, Ministry of Education, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450002, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single Li ion conducting solid-state polymer electrolytes (SLIC-SPEs) can effectively inhibit the growth of Li dendrites in Li-metal batteries. However, SLIC-SPE synthesis using traditional polymerization methods yields electrolytes with insufficient conductivity, which limits their practical application. Herein, a novel Li+ conductor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is fabricated via the pyrolysis of poly(lithium 4-styrene sulfonate) and citric acid. The large CQD anionic size and hydrogen bonding interaction with matrix hinder anion migration in the polyethylene oxide matrix and thereby grants a high Li+ transport number of 0.9446. Moreover, CQD incorporation improves the mechanical properties and ionic conductivity of the SPEs. The as-prepared SPE membrane demonstrates a high room-temperature ionic conductivity of 2.02 × 10−4 S cm−1. All-solid-state Li-metal batteries fabricated with these SPEs show good cycling stability, rate performance, and capacity retention over 1000 cycles at 2 C and 60 °C. The SPEs also withstand deformations such as bending and twisting.

A new type of CQD is synthesized by pyrolysis, and the corresponding SLIC-SPEs is also developed. The large size of CQD anions makes their migration in the polyethylene oxide (PEO) matrix difficult. The SPE possesses a high lithium ion transport number (0.9446) and room-temperature ionic conductivity of 2.02 × 10−4 S cm−1. This material shows outstanding power performance and cycling stability in a battery prototype.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Li-metal batteries (LMBs) are important the next-generation energy storage devices due to their high energy densities [1]. However, traditional commercial liquid electrolytes cannot inhibit the formation of lithium dendrites, and the flammable organic solvents pose a potential safety hazard [2,3]. These issues have hindered the development of LMBs in the automotive industry and in large-format batteries. In comparison, Solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) have many comparative advantages, including greater safety, excellent ﬂexibility and improved system energy density from their low weights [4–8]. SPEs shaped by melt processing show good interfacial contact with the Li anode, facilitated by the molten-state fluidity [9–12]. Further, flexible and wearable electronics, such as smart watches, artificial skins, implantable medical devices and epidermal sensors were bright into social life, it is urgent to develop reliable power sources with high ﬂexibility and stretchability [13,14]. Due to good flexibility and durability, SPEs have shown great potential application in ﬂexible high-density power sources [15–17].

Conventional SPEs comprise polymer hosts, such as polyethylene oxide (PEO) and lithium salts. In these dual-ion conductors, both the anions and Li+ move under an electric field. However, Li+ contributes only ~ 20% of the overall ionic current because of its low motility [18]. This results in a strong concentration gradient that has deleterious effects, such as favored dendritic growth and limited power delivery. Single lithium ion conducting SPEs (SLIC-SPEs) are able to resolve the formation of lithium dendrites and other safety problems. The common approaches to fabricate SLIC-SPEs are classified into three types: (1) covalent grafting of anions to the polymer backbone, (2) attaching anions to an inorganic backbone, and (3) adding anion acceptors to the polymer chains to limit the motion of anions [18–23].

As early as the 1980s, Bannister et al. proposed using two polyanionic addition polymers containing alkyl sulfonic acid and perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acid side groups to inhibit anion mobility in SLIC-SPEs. While the fabricated SPE had a high Li+ transport number (t Li +), the very low dissociation of carboxylate anion and Li+ led to low ion conductivities even at elevated temperatures (10−6 S cm−1 at 60 °C) [20]. On the other hand, the high room-temperature crystallinity of PEO restricts Li+ transport, further affecting ion conduction [24–26]. Bouchet et al. designed a single-ion conductor based on triblock copolymers. Poly(styrene trifluoromethanesulfonylimide of lithium) was used as the anion, because its structure allowed negative-charge delocalization; the values of t Li + and conductivity reached 0.85 and 1.3 × 10−5 S cm−1 at 60 °C, respectively [18]. Our group synthesized a single-ion polymer conductor with an alternating structure of maleic anhydride and lithium 4-styrenesulfonyl(phenylsulfonyl)imide [27]. SPEs demonstrated a high ionic conductivity and Li+ transport number (δ Li + = 3.08 × 10−4 S cm−1 and t Li + = 0.93) [28]. In order to improve the rate capability, the lithium salt was modified with ethyl glycinate, and the δ Li + reached to 1.42 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 25 °C [29]. However, despite their good electrochemical properties, the synthesis of these SPEs involves costly raw materials and complex procedures, and their mechanical properties are also poor.

Adding nanofillers to a polymer matrix is a proven effective strategy to improve the electrochemical properties and thermal stabilities of materials without reducing their mechanical properties [30–38]. Lee et al. synthesized the core-shell structured SiO2(Li+) nanoparticles with uniform spherical shape and used as functional fillers in the composite gel polymer electrolytes. The SiO2(Li+) particles are intrinsic single ion conductors, since the sulfonate anions (–SO3 −) are anchored to pendant group on the polymer around silica core. The generation of a free volume at the interface of the dispersed SiO2(Li+) nanoparticles may also contribute to the improved ionic conductivity. In addition, they also found that the t Li + and δ Li + decreased with the increase of the particle size of SiO2. This may be related to the accumulation of bulky SiO2 (> 100 nm) insulating particles that reduces the lithium ions transport channel space as well as uneven distribution of Li+ [39,40]. In hence, using a smaller nanostructured material instead of SiO2 may significantly enhance t Li + and δ Li +. For instance, Wei et al. reported a novel carbon nanostructure of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a PEO matrix, and used it to fabricate a composite solid electrolyte. Their results showed that the addition of CQDs helps reduce the crystallinity of the system and significantly improve the room temperature conductivity and ductility of SPEs [41]. However, they only added CQDs to the system as nano-filler, CQDs and Li+ conductor without closely linking, result in a low t Li +. Therefore, anchoring the anion on the surface of the CQDs is an effective method to limit the movement of the anion and increase the t Li +; while providing a large free volume at the interface may further improve the δ Li + due to the smaller particle size of CQDs (diameter < 10 nm) is more favorable for the uniform distribution of Li+ than SiO2 at the nanometer scale.

Based on these considerations, in this study, we design a novel lithium conductor based on CQDs and incorporate it with a PEO matrix to form SPEs. The CQDs were synthesized by single-step pyrolysis of poly(lithium 4-styrene sulfonate) (PLSS) and citric acid (CA), and named PLSSCQD. Unlike previous attempts to reduce the movement of anions by binding them, this method simply increases the volume of individual anions. This means that SLIC-SPEs can be prepared without complex synthesis and that different polymer matrix can be more freely selected for various application conditions. Meanwhile, the generation of a free volume at the interface of the dispersed CQDs may also contribute to the improved ionic conductivity. The addition of CQDs also reduce the crystallinity of SPEs while making the membranes more flexible, which both inhibit the growth of lithium dendrites and hinder puncturing of the electrolyte, which can cause battery short-circuit. PLSS and CA are commercially available and inexpensive, the CQDs prepared by this method are stable and efficient, and the battery cycling tests reveal superior cycle stability and power performance. The CQD SPEs also exhibit excellent environmental stability and the SPE membranes can be prepared in air. Considering the above advantages, these CQD-based SPEs are promising candidates for next-generation functional electrolytes in lithium batteries.

The preparation of synthesis of the CQDs by pyrolysis has been widely reported in the previous studies. Herein, we design a type of CQDs on the surface passivated by PLSS. Fig. 1a depicts the synthesis and molecular configuration of PLSSCQD prepared by the pyrolysis of low-cost PLSS and CA at a temperature high enough for CA–CQD conversion but too low to allow the decomposition of PLSS (Fig. S1) [42]. Thus, the main chemical structure and groups of PLSS were retained [43]. The detail is described in brief in the Supplementary material (Fig. S2).

PLSSCQD and PEO in different ratios were added to DMSO and continuously stirred at 70 °C for 12 h. The resultant homogeneous solutions were labeled PLSSCQD-x/PEO (x = 2, 4, and 6, for the PLSSCQD:PEO weight ratios of 2:10, 4:10, and 6:10, respectively). The solution was cast onto a flat Teflon plate with a doctor blade and dried for 48 h at 50 °C. Finally, the dried membranes were cut into the required shapes and sizes for the experiments. The above process was performed in atmospheric conditions. As references, LiTFSI/PEO and PLSS/PEO membranes were also fabricated by the same procedure in a glove box filled with Ar (< 0.5 ppm H2O and O2) using the molar ratio of EO:Li = 8:1. The prepared SPE membranes were punched into circles for electrochemical tests, with the diameter of 19 mm.

Batteries with the structures of LiFePO4 (LFP)|LiTFSI/PEO|Li and LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li were prepared. The LFP cathode for the LFP|LiTFSI/PEO|Li battery was fabricated by the following method. LFP, carbon black (Super-P), and PVDF were mixed (8:1:1 by weight) by ball milling to obtain a homogeneous slurry. The slurry was drop-casted onto Al foil using a doctor blade and vacuum-dried at 100 °C for 12 h to remove the residual solvent. The foil was cut into 12-mm-diameter disks for further use. For the LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li battery, the LFP cathode was prepared by the same method, except PLSSCQD-4/PEO was used as the binder and the weight ratio of LFP:SP:PLSSCQD-4/PEO was 7:1:2. Coin cells were assembled using Li metal anodes, LFP cathodes, the as-prepared electrolyte membranes, and 2032-type cell casings.

The desorption analyses were performed in a TG analyser (TA Instruments SDT Q600, America) using nitrogen flow (50 mL min−1). The temperature was increased linearly at a rate of 10 °C min−1 up to 1000 °C. The morphology and microstructure of PLSSCQD were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL 2010F, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of the aqueous PLSSCQD (0.2 wt%, w/v) were recorded on a Hitachi F-4600 ﬂuorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using a Thermo Fisher EscaLab Xi+ with an Al anode source operated at 14.4 kV, in order to analyze the chemical composition of the materials. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental maps were collected on a Hitachi S4800 SEM system. Solid-state 13C CP/MAS NMR spectra were recorded on an Agilent 600 DD2 spectrometer (Agilent, USA, magnetic field strength 14.1 T) at resonance frequency of 150.72 MHz for 13C using the cross-polarization (CP), magic-angle spinning (MAS), and a high-power 1H decoupling. The powder samples were placed in a pencil-type zirconia rotor of 4.0 mm o.d. The spectra were obtained at a spinning speed of 10 kHz(4.2 μs 90° pulses), a 2 ms CP pulse, and a recycle delay of 3 s. The C signal of tetramethylsilane (TMS) at 0 ppm was used as the reference of 13C chemical shift. Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATRFTIR) spectroscopy was performed using a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The crystal morphologies and structures of the electrolyte membranes were probed using polarized-light microscopy (PLM; Nikon ECLIPSE LV100N, Japan). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) data were recorded using a Q20 instrument (TA Instruments, USA) in a N2 atmosphere with a flow rate of 50 mL min−1 and a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 10° to 100°C. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15 nm), 2θ = 10–70°, a scan rate of 10° min−1, a voltage of 40 kV, and a current of 200 mA. The strain and puncture tests of the SPE membranes were tested on a tensile machine (UTM2203, SUNS, China). In strain testing, the stretching speed of testing machine was 10 mm/min and the sample length was 40 mm, width and thickness were 5 mm and 80 µm. In puncture testing, the sample was cut into a disc with 80 µm thickness and 20 mm diameter, the speed of testing machine was 10 mm/min with a 1 mm diameter puncture needle. The stoichiometry was determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110).

The ionic conductivities of the SPE membranes were measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a perturbation of 50 mV in the frequency range of 8 MHz to 0.1 Hz at various temperatures between 25 and 80 °C. Cells with the configuration of SS|SPE membrane|SS were assembled to test their resistances. Impedance data were obtained on the electrochemical workstation (Zahner, Zennium Pro, Germany). The ionic conductivity (δ Li +, S cm−1) was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
δ
Li
+
=
L
R
b
× S
where L (cm) is the thickness of the SPE membrane, R
b (Ω) is the bulk resistance, and S (cm2) is the area of the SPE membrane.

Linear-sweep voltammetry was performed using the cell configuration of Li|SPE membrane| stainless steel (SS). The experiments were implemented at room temperature from 1 to 6 V (versus Li+/Li) at a scan rate of 1 mV·s−1 on an electrochemical workstation.The polarization tests employed a symmetric cell configuration of Li|SPE membrane|Li on a LAND battery test system (CT2001A, LANHE, China) for 2 h during each process at the constant current density of 0.2, 0.5, or 1 mA cm−2 at 60 °C. The electrochemical floating experiments were conducted with NCM523|SPE membrane|Li cells, which were first charged to 4.0 V and then held at progressively higher voltages for 10 h in each step at 60 °C. Finally, The LFP|SPE membranes|Li cells were assembled for testing the galvanostatic charge–discharge cycling performance and C-rate capacity, using the LAND battery test system in a voltage window of 2.0–3.8 V at 60 °C.

In order to prove the successful preparation of CQDs, we observed the morphology of the samples and verified their photoluminescence properties. TEM images of PLSSCQD showed well-dispersed spherical particles with an average size of 2.90 nm (Fig. 1b) and a normal size distribution (Fig. 1c). Here, the anions are CQDs with diameters of 1.8–4.2 nm, exceeding those of traditional anions by ~10 × (the effective radii of typical diffusing species are 0.229–0.402 nm) [44,45]. Steric hindrance from the large size of CQDs means that these anions are effectively fixed in the polymer matrix, thereby realizing single Li+ transport. In addition, hydrogen bonding interaction and other strong interactions between oxygen functional groups of CQDs and PEO also hinder anion migration. The CQD anions fixed on the PEO segments are difficult to move freely, so the Li+ transport number is increased. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1b) of the CQDs displayed identical and well-resolved lattice fringes, with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphene carbon. Fig. 1d showed the excitation-dependent emission behavior of the PLSSCQD. The red-shift of the emission maximum with increasing excitation wavelength is accord with CQD, as reported in the literature [33]. XPS (Fig. 1e) was utilized to measure the elemental composition and the chemical state of CQDs. The CQDs contained 31.06 at% carbon, 64.08 at% oxygen, and 4.86 at% sulfur. Analogous results were demonstrated in the EDS pattern (Fig. S3 and Table S1). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum can be resolved into four peaks ( Fig. 2a), which are assigned to sp2 carbon atoms (284.4 eV), alcoholic species (C–O, 285.3 eV), carbonyl species (C˭O, 288.4 eV), and C–S/Caromatic (289.1 eV). Fig. 2b presented the S 2p spectrum of PLSSCQD with two major peaks at 167.8 and 168.8 eV, characteristic of the 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 states of S in the –SO2 −. Two minor fitted peaks at 168.4 and 169.3 eV are assigned to –SO3 −, which originated from the sulfonic acid groups of PSS [45–47]. In order to further determine the molecular structure, resonances of the PLSS and PLSSCQD are resolved in the solidstate 13C CP-MAS NMR spectra. The peaks with chemical shift values of 140 and 127 ppm are due to the aromatic carbons of the PLSS, and these two peaks still exist in the NMR spectra of PLSSCQD (Fig. S4) [48]. The XPS and NMR results further confirmed the presence of the sulfonate anions (–SO3 −) are anchored to pendant group on the polymer around CQD core. This structure is formed by surface passivation or/and functionalization. In this system, PLSS are the passivation agents. There are many oxygen groups, i.e., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, and also surface defects on the surface of CDs. This provides extensive anchors for binding of the passivation agents. There are strong PLSS adsorption on the surface of CQDs through their interactions of oxygen-containing functional groups [49,50].

The as-prepared SPE membranes were flexible and stretchable, and their thicknesses can be adjusted by controlling the concentration of mother liquor. Fig. 2c illustrated the FTIR spectra of PLSSCQD and SPEs. Compared with PEO membrane, PLSSCQD presented different FTIR characteristics, five new peaks at 1410, 1193, 1010, 770, 680 cm−1 appeared. The absorbance peaks of 1193 and 1010 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of –SO3 − group of the PLSSCQD. The absorption band at 1410 cm−1 was considered as the aromatic –C˭C– stretching vibrations. The two peaks at 770 and 680 cm−1 attributed to =C–H out of plane deformation vibrations [51–55]. These five characteristic peaks also can be observed in PLSSCQD/PEO FTIR spectrum. These results indicated that the present of PLSSCQD in SPEs. In addition, Fig. S5 showed the FTIR spectra of the hydroxyl stretching (v O–H) region (3100–3700 cm−1) of PLSSCQD and PLSSCQD/PEO. A broad v O-H band was observed due to variety of intra-hydrogen bonds in PLSSCQD. The PLSSCQD FTIR spectrum had a broad band center at ~ 3434 cm−1. A obvious difference of the v O–H region was observed in PLSSCQD/PEO FTIR spectrum, the broad center band shifted to lower wavenumber, which appeared at ~ 3351 cm−1, and the shape of the v O-H region became broader. The results suggested the formation of strong hydrogen bonds between the PLSSCQD and PEO [56]. EDS mapping results of PLSSCQD/PEO SPEs surface revealed that C, O, and S were homogeneously distributed, which further confirmed the uniform PLSSCQD dispersion in the PEO matrix (Fig. 2d). Cross-sectional SEM and EDS mapping images demonstrated the SPE with a 80.8 µm width and uniform PLSSCQD dispersion in the PEO matrix (Fig. 2e). PLM identified crystalline and amorphous components in the SPE membrane at the micrometer scale. The pure PEO polymer matrix exhibited larger spherulites, with the optical microscope the Maltese cross patterns observed in Fig. 3a [57]. The image of the PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE membrane (Fig. 3b) showed few any representative spherulites, the amorphous regions were larger, the right-angled intersection extinction phenomenon was disappeared. These results proved that the addition of PLSSCQD effectively inhibited formation of large-sized spherulites in the SPE membrane. However, the image of PLSSCQD-6/PEO SPE membrane again showed spherulites, possibly because the excess PLSSCQD would agglomerate in the matrix and act as a nucleating agent (Fig. S6). Moreover, DSC was used to quantitatively characterize the crystallinity of SPEs (χ c). The DSC curves and the corresponding data are shown in Fig. 3c and Table 1, respectively. The χ c of all SPEs were calculated using Eq. (1). ΔH
m is the melting enthalpy of the membrane, ΔH PEO (196.4 J g−1) is the enthalpy of fusion for fully crystalline PEO [58], and f PEO is the weight fraction of PEO in the SPE sample:
(2)
χ
c
=
Δ
H
m
Δ H
PEO
f
PEO
× 100 %

According to Table 1, pure PEO had a melting temperature (T m) of 67.7 °C and χ c of 69.9%. The χ c decreased with added PLSSCQD, being 52.1% and 29.9% for PLSSCQD-2/PEO and PLSSCQD-4/PEO, respectively. This significant decrease in crystallinity may be caused by disrupting the regularity of PEO matrix segments. It is worth mentioning that adding PLSSCQD also resulted in lower T m values (59.1 °C and 54.2 °C for PLSSCQD-2/PEO and PLSSCQD-4/PEO, respectively). However, for PLSSCQD-6/PEO, the crystallinity was increased to 50.3%. The same trends appeared in the XRD patterns (Fig. S7). A lower χ c may improve the δ Li + of SPE by facilitating the movement of EO chain segment, while a lower T m indicated a broader temperature range for material use. On the other hand, the interlaced EO chain segments inhibited the movement of CQD anions, but the smaller Li+ could be transported by the EO chain segments (Fig. 3d). Furthermore, the weak interaction between Li+ and CQD anions mean that the CQDs cannot strongly interfere with the movement of Li+. As a result, t Li + can be increased without decreasing δ Li +.

In a solid battery, the SPE functioned as the intermediate material between the positive and negative electrodes. However, the internal structure of the battery may change under an external force. This problem can be avoided by using SPEs with good mechanical properties. Moreover, good flexibility of the SPE is important in large-scale production for reducing the processing and storage costs. Considering these requirements, a series of abuse tests was performed to characterize the mechanical performance of PLSSCQD SPEs. After folding, coiling, and twisting, the unfolded PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE is restored to its initial condition ( Fig. 4a). In the tensile test (Fig. 4b), the strain first increased when the ratio of PLSSCQD:PEO increased from 2:10 to 4:10, but decreased sharply at 6:10. This may be attributed to the PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE having the lowest crystallinity a, and thus the greatest toughness. With further increasing the ratio of PLSSCQD, the PLSSCQDs were incompletely dispersed in the PEO matrix; the formed aggregates may induce PEO crystallization, thereby reduce the toughness of the SPE. However, when the PLSSCQD content was increased from 2:10 to 4:10, the crystallinity of SPE decreased and the strength were decreased. At a ratio of 6:10, although the crystallinity increased, the PLSSCQD aggregates formed many stress-concentration points in the SPE membrane, thereby reducing stress. Compared with the pure PEO membrane with maximum stress and strain of ~ 6.9 MPa and ~ 1.89, respectively, the PLSSCQD-4/PEO film has a lower maximum stress (~ 5.1 MPa) and a higher strain (223%). Fig. 4c showed the puncture resistance of the SPEs. The maximum strain and stress were observed in PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE (strain ~ 6448%, stress ~ 1.3 MPa), since the SPE with the lowest crystallinity (i.e., the most flexibility) was able to withstand a larger impact load. The superior puncture resistance of the PLSSCQD-4/PEO membrane indicated that it can remain mechanically stable during the battery pack assembly process and resist the damage from dendrites.

To verify the usability of the developed SPEs in high-performance rechargeable batteries, their ionic conductivities were measured by EIS. For comparison, three other SPEs of lithium bis(triﬂuoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI)/PEO, composites prepared by directly combining PLSS and CQD/PEO (PLSS-CQD composites/PEO) and PLSS/PEO were also prepared. The temperature-dependent δ Li + of SPE membranes at 25–80 °C was shown in Fig. 5a (details in Fig. S8). The results show that PLSSCQD-4/PEO membrane exhibits the highest δ Li + (2.02 × 10−4 S cm–1) at room temperature. When the temperature was higher than 50 °C, the δ Li + of SPEs increased abruptly, which may be attributed to the enhanced capacity for Li+ transport after the PEO crystallites melt [55]. In addition, at any given temperature, the SPEs δ Li + increased with increasing PLSSCQD content, but for PLSSCQD-6/PEO, the δ Li + of SPEs decreased because of the agglomerated PLSSCQD in the PEO matrix. To investigate the t Li + of PLSSCQD/PEO SPEs, the AC impedance spectra before and after polarization and the variations in current with time were measured (Fig. 5b). t Li + could be calculated according to Eq. (3): [28].
(3)
t
Li
+
=
I
s
(
Δ V − I
0
R
0
)
I
0
(
Δ V − I
s
R
s
)
where ΔV is the potential applied across the cell, I
0 and R
0 represent the initial current and resistance, and I
s and R
s are the steady-state current and resistance after polarization for 10800 s, respectively. The calculated t Li + value for the PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE was 0.9446, this result is extremely high compared with the other SPEs (details in Fig. S9).

The electrochemical stability window is a critical parameter for the practical application of electrolytes in LMBs. The decomposition onset potential of PLSSCQD-4/PEO is ~ 4.4 V, exceeding those of LiTFSI/PEO, PLSS/PEO, PLSSCQD-2/PEO, and PLSSCQD-6/PEO at approximately 4.1, 3.82, 4.08, and 4.15 V, respectively (Fig. 5c). To measure the oxidative stability of PLSSCQD/PEO SPEs more accurately, an electrochemical floating experiment was performed (Fig. 5d). As a more stringent test for the electrochemical stability window, the measured leakage current can be directly used to evaluate the applicability of SPEs in LMBs. PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE showed a small leakage current (< 10 μA) at < 4.2 V. In comparison, the leakage current of LiTFSI/PEO SPE exceeded 1 mA at 4.2 V. These results implied that adding the right amount of PLSSCQD can widen the electrochemical stability window, possibly because of the good electrode–SPE interfacial compatibility. To verify the best Li/EO molar ratio of PLSSCQD/PEO, we measured ratio of Li element in PLSSCQD by ICP-OES. The Li/EO ratio of PLSSCQD-x (x = 2, 4, 6)/PEO were 1:25.03, 1:12.51 and 1:8.34, respectively. The results indicated that PLSSCQD/PEO SPEs with the best electrochemical performance when the Li/EO ratio is 1:12.51.

The stability of the electrode–SPE interface is a critical factor to influence the cycling performance of LMBs. Li plating/stripping experiments in symmetric cells were performed for investigating the dynamic stability of the electrode–SPE interface. Fig. 5e showed the voltage time curve with discharge–charge cycling for 1 h with a constant current density of 0.2, 0.5, or 1 mA cm−2 at 60 °C. The PLSSCQD-4/PEO symmetric lithium cell displayed long-term stability under 0.2 and 0.5 mA cm−2. In contrast, the LiTFSI/PEO cell showed strong polarization between Li stripping and plating, and it could not be operated for > 50 h. These results clearly demonstrate a good compatibility between a PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE and a Li-metal electrode.

Next, we demonstrated the feasibility of the as-prepared PLSSCQD SPE membranes in LMBs. We used two SPE membranes for discharge–charge testing: PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE, which showed the best electrochemical and mechanical properties, and LiTFSI/PEO as a reference. The corresponding solid-state LMBs (LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li and LFP|LiTFSI/PEO|Li, named PLSSCQDLMB and LiTFSILMB, respectively) were assembled, and their cell performances were measured at 60 °C. As shown in Fig. 6a, the curves of PLSSCQDLMB displayed flat discharge plateaus at 3.43 V and high reversibility at a small discharge–charge rate of 0.1 C. Moreover, at the increased discharge–charge rate to 0.2, 0.5 and 2 C, the discharge plateaus remained stable, and the voltage differed only slightly from that of the charge plateaus (Fig. S10). This indicated that the SPEs have low polarization during charge and discharge, which is conducive to stable and efficient operation of LMBs. Fig. 6b presented typical charge-discharge curves of PLSSCQDLMB battery at various rates from 0.1 to 2 C at 60 °C. The PLSSCQDLMB delivered a highest discharge capacity at 0.1 C; and discharge capacity decreased with the increase of rate. In the cyclic discharge–charge tests, PLSSCQDLMB delivered an initial discharge capacity of 155.9 mAh g−1 with a capacity retention rate of 94.3% after 100 cycles at a rate of 0.2 C (Fig. 6c). Excellent performance was also observed at 0.1, 0.5, and 2 C. The corresponding initial capacities for LMBs are 160.4, 146.6, and 136.7 mAh g−1; and the retention rates are 96.3%, 91.2%, and 83.4% after 50, 300, and 1000 cycles at 0.2, 0.5 and 2 C, respectively (Fig. S11). In contrast, LiTFSILMB showed a lower initial capacity (130.7 mAh g−1) and capacity retention (56.5% after 100 cycles, Fig. 6c) at 0.2 C. In addition, the coulombic efficiency of PLSSCQDLMB remained stable and higher than that of LiTFSILMB after 100 cycles. Compared with other reported SLIC-SPE, our SPEs showed ultrahigh cycling performance and specific capacity at a high C-rate, suggesting a long working life and stability (Table S2). This is ascribed to the high δ Li + and t Li + of the PLSSCQD SPE. During the rate capability test (Fig. 6d), the specific capacity of the cell decreased with increasing discharge–charge rate, with values of 168, 158, 140, 133, and 124 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively. Moreover, after high-speed charging and discharging cycles at 5 C (79 mAh g−1, Fig. 6d), the specific capacity of the cell almost returned to near its initial values of (~ 165 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C and then ~ 154 mAh g−1 at 0.2 C). For LiTFSILMB, the coulombic efficiency decays rapidly. This behavior attributes to the continuous reduction of the SPEs by the formation of inactive (‘dead’) Li during each cycle [59], thickens the SEI on Li, and limits access to the electrodeposited Li metal [11]. In comparison, PLSSCQDLMB shows high Coulombic efficiency (~ 100%, Fig. S11d), even after 1000 cycles. Although the coulombic efficiency of PLSSCQDLMB remained stable and higher than that of LiTFSILMB after 100 cycles, it is still less than 100%. This phenomenon may be due to the inevitable decomposition of dissociative PLSS during cycling test, whose decomposition onset potential of PLSS is ~ 3.82 V (Fig. 5c). The outstanding rate capability performance indicates that LMBs using PLSSCQD-based SPE could adapt to a variety of discharge–charge rates (0.1–5 C). Such high coulombic efficiency, long service life, high rate performance, and other excellent characteristics suggest the suitability of this new PLSSCQD SPE for all-solid-state LMBs.

The surface morphologies of Li metal (after 100 cycles of discharge–charge at a current density of 0.2 C) was observed by SEM ( Fig. 7). For the cell using LiTFSI/PEO SPE, mossy Li dendritic deposits appeared after 100 cycles (Fig. 7c). This morphology evolution can be attributed to the concentration polarization in the electrolyte. In contrast, while that using PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE only showed loose electrodeposited Li after 100 cycles (Fig. 7b). This is in very good agreement with the electrochemical results, suggesting a stabilized interface between the SLIC-SPE and the Li metal electrode, and the formation of lithium dendrites can be effectively inhibited.

Finally, PLSSCQDLMB all-solid-state coin cell and pouch cell were assembled and subjected to abuse testing. After twisting and unfolding, the PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE was able to regain its original shape and functioned properly within a PLSSCQDLMB coin cell ( Fig. 8a). After bending, the pouch cell remained stable and safe, and a connected LED bulb remained working (Fig. 8b, Video S1). Thus, PLSSCQD-4/PEO satisfies the safety requirements for the production and application of all-solid-state LMBs.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2020.105698.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video 1.
Video 1
.

In summary, SLIC-SPE membranes with different PLSSCQD contents were prepared from safe and low-cost materials without using complex chemical synthesis steps. The PLSSCQD could be produced in larger batches than those possible by conventional methods. The large anion size in the PLSSCQD/PEO membrane and the low crystallization enhanced δ Li + and t Li +. Among the prepared membranes, the one with PLSSCQD:PEO = 4:10 (w/w) had the highest room-temperature δ Li + (2.02 × 10−4 S cm−1 at room temperature) and a high t Li + of 0.9446. Cyclic testing of the symmetric Li cell demonstrated that this membrane effectively suppressed Li dendrite growth and operated at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. Furthermore, solid-state LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li batteries exhibited excellent electrochemical performance with ultrahigh cycling stability, capacity retention, rate capability, and stable coulombic efficiency. Finally, the PLSSCQD-4/PEO SPE had favorable mechanical properties and an LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li all-solid-state cell worked stably during bending and unbending. Thus, this new CQD-based Li conductor/PEO composite SPE is promising for developing safe and high-energy-density solid-state Li batteries.

Zeyu Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft preparation. Feng Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Shao shan Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Fei Zhai: Validation, Formal analysis. Yu Li: Supervision, Visualization. Yiyu Feng: Supervision. Wei Feng: Supervision, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Doped carbon nanoparticles as a new platform for highly photoluminescent dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zwitterionic impetus on single lithium-ion conduction in solid polymer electrolyte for all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Lu, G. Li, Y. Yu, X. Gao, L. Zheng, Z. Chen, Zwitterionic impetus on single lithium-ion conduction in solid polymer electrolyte for all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries, Chemical Engineering Journal 384 (2020) 123237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High performance composite polymer electrolytes for lithium-ion polymer cells composed of a graphite negative electrode and LiFePO4 positive electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-S. Lee, W.-K. Shin, J. S. Kim, D.-W. Kim, High performance composite polymer electrolytes for lithium-ion polymer cells composed of a graphite negative electrode and LiFePO4positive electrode, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 18359‐18366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A single-ion conducting polymer electrolyte based on poly(lithium 4-styrenesulfonate) for high-performance lithium metal batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Chen, C. Niu, Y. Chen, W. Shang, Y. Qu, Z. Du, L. Zhao, X. Liao, J. Du, Y. Chen, A single-ion conducting polymer electrolyte based on poly(lithium 4-styrenesulfonate) for high-performance lithium metal batteries, Solid State Ionics 341 (2019) 115048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A metal organic framework derived solid electrolyte for lithium–sulfur batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Chiochan, X. Yu, M. Sawangphruk, A. Manthiram, A Metal Organic Framework Derived Solid Electrolyte for Lithium–Sulfur Batteries, Adv. Energy Mater. 10 (2020) 2001285.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly conductive PEDOT:PSS electrode by simple film treatment with methanol for ITO-free polymer solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Alemu, H.-Y. Wei, K.-C. Ho, C.-W. Chu, Highly conductive PEDOT:PSS electrode by simple film treatment with methanol for ITO-free polymer solar cells, Energy Environ. Sci. 5 (2012) 9662.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Miscibility and hydrogen bonding in blends of poly(ethylene oxide) and Kraft lignin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. F. Kadla, S. Kubo, Miscibility and Hydrogen Bonding in Blends of Poly(ethylene oxide) and Kraft Lignin, Macromolecules 36 (2003) 7803‐7811.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crystalline phases, morphology and conductivity of PEO:LiTFSI electrolytes in the eutectic region

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Marzantowicz, J. Dygas, F. Krok, J. Nowiński, A. Tomaszewska, Z. Florjańczyk, E. Zygadło-Monikowska, Crystalline phases, morphology and conductivity of PEO:LiTFSI electrolytes in the eutectic region, Journal of Power Sources 159 (2006) 420‐430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A flexible ceramic/polymer hybrid solid electrolyte for solid‐state lithium metal batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Pan, L. Zhang, W. Qian, X. Wu, K. Dong, H. Zhang, S. Zhang, A Flexible Ceramic/Polymer Hybrid Solid Electrolyte for Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries, Adv. Mater. 32 (2020) 2000399.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantifying inactive lithium in lithium metal batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Fang, J. Li, M. Zhang, Y. Zhang, F. Yang, J. Z. Lee, M.-H. Lee, J. Alvarado, M. A. Schroeder, Y. Yang, Quantifying inactive lithium in lithium metal batteries, Nature 572 (2019) 511‐515.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zeyu Li received his BS degree in Chemistry from Zhengzhou University (China) in 2015 and completed his MS degree under the supervision of Prof. Kun Dai at Zhengzhou University (China) in 2017. He is now pursuing Ph.D. under the tutelage of Prof. Wei Feng at the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University. Currently, his research is focused on carbon dots and solid polymer electrolytes.

Feng Liu is currently a master of polymer materials and engineering in the Functional Organic Carbon Composites Laboratory of Tianjin University, under the supervision of Professor Wei Feng. His research interests include the synthesis, photoluminescence mechanism and applications of carbon dots.

Shaoshan Chen received his BS degree in Polymer Science and Engineering from Beijing University of Chemical Technology (China) in 2014. He joined in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University to pursue Ph.D. with Prof. Wei Feng and Yiyu Feng in 2018 on solid polymer electrolytes for lithium metal batteries.

Fei Zhai received his BS degree in Chemistry from Jilin University (China) in 2009 and completed his MS degree under the supervision of Prof. Haizhu Sun at Northeast Normal University (China) and State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials (Jilin University, China). He is now pursuing Ph.D. under the tutelage of Prof. Wei Feng at the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University. Currently, his research is focused on photo-responsive molecules and polymers, modified graphene, and nanocomposites applied for solar-thermal fuels and stimuli-response.

Yu Li is a lecturer at the School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree from the same department of Tianjin University in 2011 and he worked as a postdoctoral research fellow at Tianjin University from 2011 to 2014. His current research is focused on conductive polymer and nano energy storage materials.

Yiyu Feng is a professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering at Tianjin University. He obtained his Ph.D. from Tianjin University in 2009 and held an academic position at Tianjin University. He has authored and co-authored over 100 academic articles and reviews. Currently, his research is focused on carbon-based materials or composites for solar-thermal fuels, interfacial heat dissipation and structural self-healing.

Wei Feng is a professor at the School of Materials Science and Engineering in Tianjin University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree from the Xi’an Jiaotong University (China) in 2000. Then, he worked at Osaka University and Tsinghua University as a JSPS fellow and postdoctoral researcher, respectively. In 2004, he became a full professor at Tianjin University. He has obtained the support of the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars in China. His research interests include photo-responsive organic molecules and their derivatives, thermal-conductive and high-strength carbon-based composites, and two-dimensional fluorinated carbon materials and polymers.
